◆ Man Made Alive · Introductory Series · Video 2 of 3

The stories you
never chose.

Three cultural visions of manhood have been steeping into you without your permission. None of them begins with God — and that single missing anchor is why willpower can't fix what's actually broken.

Recognition, not condemnation Three default stories Each one fails differently All three share one flaw
The jolt

The moment that
stops a man cold.

At some point — maybe recently, maybe years ago — you had a moment that stopped you cold. Not guilt exactly. Something more unsettling than guilt: the sense that something you didn't choose was running underneath you, shaping you, and you had only just caught a glimpse of it.

JOLT 01

The words you swore
you'd never say

You hear yourself say something to your kids — and recognize, with a jolt, that it was exactly what your father used to say to you. The same words. The same tone. You hadn't chosen those words. They were already in you, waiting.

JOLT 02

The car in
the driveway

You react to your wife in a way you swore you never would. Afterwards you sit in the car in the driveway and feel something shift. Not guilt — something more unsettling than guilt. The feeling of having just brushed up against a story that's been writing your life for years.

That moment has a name.

The tea bag

The water doesn't
decide what colour it becomes.

Drop a tea bag into a cup of hot water and walk away. Come back ten minutes later, and the water is no longer clear. It's taken on color, depth, flavor — and it did all of that without any intention. Without any decision on the water's part. The bag was placed there, and the water simply absorbed what was in it.

That is what culture does to a man's understanding of manhood. The stories were placed there — by a father, by peers, by failure, by the thousand small messages absorbed from a lifetime of watching how the world rewards and punishes different kinds of men. They didn't ask permission. They didn't announce themselves. They simply seeped in. And over time, they began to shape the way a man thinks, leads, relates, and measures himself — long before he had the chance to decide whether they were worth absorbing.

Most men have never stopped to ask which stories they actually chose. And which ones chose them.
The baseline problem

Without a baseline,
any story sounds true.

Think about how a lie detector test works. Before the examiner asks a single hard question, he asks a series of simple ones — your name, where you live, basic facts you already know. Not because he doesn't know the answers. Because he needs a baseline. A standard of what truth looks like in your body. Once that baseline is established, everything else gets measured against it.

◆ With a baseline

Hard questions can be answered

Once a calibrated standard is set, everything else gets measured against it. The hard questions only make sense because there is an anchor point that doesn't move.

▲ Without a baseline

Any confident story sounds true

Most men received cultural visions of manhood before they had any framework to evaluate them. Without an anchored standard, any story that presents itself with enough confidence passes for true.

Story 01 of 03

Soften &
step back.

The first story tells a man that strength is dangerous, leadership is inherently oppressive, and the wisest thing he can do is keep small. In this story, assertiveness becomes aggression. Conviction becomes judgment. Any man who takes up space is suspect until proven otherwise.

Walking away from a moment when leadership was needed.
PASSIVE — THE WITHDRAWN MAN

The man present in the room.
Missing from the moment.

Avoidance is not the same as wisdom. Peacekeeping is not the same as peace.

The appeal is real. A man shaped by this story is often kind, agreeable, easy to be around. He has learned not to impose. People like him. He keeps the peace.

▲ The feeling that comes with this story

Staying quiet in a moment when something in you knew exactly what needed to be said — and saying nothing. The hollow afterward. The way you replay it later and wonder what would have happened if you'd spoken.

Or the slow realization that the people in your home have stopped bringing the hard things to you. Not because everything is fine. Because somewhere along the way they learned not to expect you to hold it.

◆ Promises
Peace. Through avoidance, accommodation, keeping the volume low.
▲ Produces
Absence with the volume turned down. A man present in the room, missing from the moment — and a family that has learned to work around him.
That's not peace. That's absence with the volume turned down.
Story 02 of 03

Harden &
prove.

The second story moves in the opposite direction. It tells a man that strength must be proven — through performance, control, and visible success. In this story, leadership means asserting authority. Provision means personal achievement. Vulnerability is a liability to manage, not a reality to share.

A room full of his presence — and empty of others.
DOMINANT — THE PROVING MAN

Surrounded by people.
Known by none of them.

Strength weaponized in service of self-preservation.

A man shaped by this story often appears confident, capable, in control. He produces results. People respect him. The room responds when he speaks.

▲ The feeling beneath this story

Being in a room full of people who admire you — and realizing that not one of them actually knows you. The way a conversation stops when you walk in. Not because you've done anything wrong — because your presence carries a weight that makes honesty feel unsafe for everyone in the room, including yourself.

Or the moment late at night when the performance finally goes quiet, and what's left is a man who has spent everything proving his worth to people whose approval he can't quite trust. The success starts to feel like a cage.

◆ Promises
Respect. Through performance, control, and visible achievement.
▲ Produces
Loneliness that accumulates. Strength that exists for the self. Relationships that go transactional. A cage made of his own results.
Dominance promises respect. It produces a man surrounded by people and known by none of them.
Story 03 of 03

Erase the
category.

The third story is newer — but spreading fast. It tells a man that masculinity itself is the problem. That male strength is inherently dangerous. The solution offered is not the reformation of manhood — it's the removal of it altogether.

Standing at the threshold — neither in nor out.
ERASED — THE APOLOGETIC MAN

A waterless cloud.
Promising rain. Delivering nothing.

Strength held back, second-guessed, and quietly grieving its own absence.

Waterless cloud · Jude 1:12 — A man shaped by this story doesn't become passive or dominant. He becomes present in the sky, promising rain, delivering nothing. He shows up. The strength meant to nourish the people around him simply evaporates before it ever reaches the ground.

A man shaped by this story doesn't become passive or dominant. He becomes unsure. The accumulated weight of a thousand messages telling him his instinct is suspect.

▲ The specific feeling of erasure

Having an instinct and immediately questioning whether you're allowed to trust it. You sense that someone in the room needs protection, you feel the pull to step toward it, and then something stops you. Not wisdom — doubt. So you hesitate. You qualify. You hold back something that was meant to be offered.

And the people around you feel the absence even if they can't name it.

◆ Promises
Safety from harm. If the strength is the problem, removing it is the solution.
▲ Produces
Men who cannot offer what those around them most need — and who carry a low, chronic grief about it that they can't quite explain.
The hunger men feel when someone simply tells them their identity matters — like they had been thirsty for a long time — is the evidence of what erasure produces.
“

At first glance, these three stories look like opposites. But a single flaw runs through all of them.

None of them begins with God.

Against the grain

Living against
your design.

Each story defines manhood without reference to the One who designed it — without asking what a man was actually made for, what he was built to carry, what his strength was originally intended to do. And here is what happens when you cut against the grain of how something was designed.

◆ With your design

Effort and design move in the same direction. The wood is formed. The work shapes rather than splinters. There is friction in the right places only — and the friction is productive.

▲ Against your design

The wood doesn't stop you. It just splinters rather than shapes. A man living against the grain of his God-given design doesn't collapse immediately either — he just frays slowly, in ways willpower cannot repair. The problem isn't the effort. It's the direction.

The exhaustion

The tiredness
has a name.

If you've been living by one of these stories — and every man watching has been, to some degree — you may already feel what comes next. A specific kind of tiredness. The exhaustion is not from the effort. It's from the friction of moving against your own design for long enough that the splintering has become familiar.

◆ The honest naming
A man working hard inside a story that
keeps demanding more and delivering less.

That is not a personal failure. It is the natural consequence of building on a foundation that was never solid enough to hold the weight of a real life. The lie detector test was running with no baseline. Everything got measured against a standard that was never anchored to truth.

Three quiet acts of recognition

What to do
before Video 3.

Not a performance. Not a fix. Three small acts of recognition — the work of a man who is, as the close puts it, "done living by default."

◆ This week

Three quiet acts
before the next video.

  1. Recall the most recent jolt. The moment that stopped you cold. The words that came out unbidden. The conversation that left a residue. Don't fix it — just recognize it. Naming it is the first move.
  2. Pick the story that steeped into you most. Passive, dominant, or erased. Don't pick the answer that flatters you — pick the one that explains the most of your life. Which feeling did you recognize?
  3. Tell one trusted man what you've recognized. A friend, a brother, a pastor. Don't ask him to fix it. Don't ask for advice. Just let it be heard. Speaking it once breaks the spell that secrecy keeps over it.
▶ Up next · Video 3 of 3 · The Man You Were Made to Become

The man you were
made to become.

Look at the water now.

You didn't choose the colour it became. But now you can see it — and seeing it honestly is the first act of a man who is done living by default. In the final video we look at the only pattern actually worth building a life around — neither passive, nor dominant, nor erased. A man whose strength was real, but always under the direction of the Father's will, always spent for the good of others. The man you were made to become.

Until then — stay steady.
